22: YHWH qanani reshit darko qedem mifalav meaz: 'The LORD possessed/created me at the beginning of his work, the first of his acts of old.' The verb qanah is disputed: it can mean 'to acquire/possess' (so most LXX manuscripts, Aquila, Theodotion) or 'to create' (so the Arian controversy reading, applied to prove the Son was a created being). The Nicene theology responded: even if qanah means 'created', Proverbs 8 is personified Wisdom literature — a poetic device, not a literal description of a divine person's ontology. The NT applies Prov 8's Wisdom-portrait to the eternal Son (Col 1:15-17; Heb 1:2-3; John 1:1-3) not to prove the Son is created, but to show that the pre-existent Son is the referent of the Wisdom-personification: the figure the wisdom tradition was groping toward in poetic imagery became flesh in Christ.
1: halo'-chokmah tiqra' — “does wisdom not call out?” The rhetorical question marks the shift from the father’s warning about the seductress (ch 7) to Wisdom’s own voice. Chokmah (H2451) and tevunah (understanding, H8394) are the two primary wisdom terms in Proverbs, frequently paired. Their personification as a woman calling in public inverts the adulteress’s lurking presence in ch 7: Wisdom is not secret but public, not hidden but crying aloud at the highest and busiest points of the city.
2: be-rosh meromim 'alei-derekh — “on the heights above the road.” Wisdom positions herself at the meromim (heights, elevated places), the road intersections, and the city gates — the three most public locations in an ancient city. The gate (sha'ar, v3) is the site of legal proceedings, commercial transactions, and public assemblies. Wisdom addresses the city at its points of highest traffic and greatest civic importance.
3: le-yad she'arim le-fi-qaret — “beside the gates, at the entrance to the city.” She'arim (gates) in the plural encompasses the multiple city gates; pi-qaret (lit. ‘the mouth of the city’) is a phrase for the main city gate as the public mouth through which the city speaks and conducts its life. Wisdom plants herself at the ‘mouth of the city’ — occupying the position of authoritative public speech.
4: 'aleichem 'ishim 'eqra' — “to you, O men ('ishim), I am calling.” 'Ishim (H376 plural) is the masculine human; the parallel bene 'adam (sons/children of Adam, H120) broadens the address to all humanity without restriction. The universal address of ch 8 is in deliberate contrast to the adulteress’s targeted seduction of one vulnerable young man; Wisdom calls everyone, without discriminating by social status.
5: havu peta'im 'ormah — “O simple ones, learn prudence.” Pethi (simple, H6612) and 'evil (fool, H191) are addressed directly; Wisdom does not exclude the most morally unformed. 'Ormah (prudence, H6195) is the quality of practical wisdom that distinguishes the skilled from the naive; lev (heart, understanding) is what the morally empty are called to receive. The invitation is to transformation, not merely to information.
6: shim'u ki-negidim 'adaber — “hear, for I will speak excellent/noble things.” Negidim (H5057) normally means ‘leaders, princes, nobles’; here as an adverb it means ‘excellent, princely, worthy things.’ The opening of wisdom’s discourse claims for itself the dignity of princely speech. Umiftach sefatai mesharim — “and the opening of my lips will be right things” (mesharim, uprightness/equity). The twin claim: noble content and right character.
7: emet yehegeh chikki — “my palate meditates truth.” 'Emet (H571, truth/faithfulness) is the first named quality of wisdom’s speech. The verb hagah (H1897, to meditate, mutter, moan — used of the sound a lion makes over its prey and of one who meditates the Torah day and night in Ps 1:2) implies that truth is not merely spoken but internalized and continually turned over. To'avat sefatai resha' — “wickedness is an abomination to my lips”: to'avah (abomination) is the strongest term for moral revulsion in the OT.
8: be-tzedeq kol-'imrei-fi — “all the words of my mouth are in righteousness.” Tzedeq (H6664, righteousness, justice) applied to speech means that wisdom’s discourse aligns with the moral order of the created world. 'Ein bahem niftal ve-'iqesh — “there is nothing twisted or crooked in them.” Niftal (twisted, H6617) and 'iqesh (H6141, crooked, perverse) are the two terms for speech that deviates from straight moral truth. Wisdom’s self-description as the straight-speaking voice is the counterpart to the seductress’s chelqat sefatayim (smooth/flattering lips, 7:21).
9: kullam nekochim le-meivin — “they are all straightforward to the one who understands.” Nakach (H5228, straight, correct, right before one) is used elsewhere of direct, unmediated confrontation with reality. The condition “to the one who understands” (meivin) introduces the epistemological circularity of wisdom: wisdom’s speech is plain to those who already have wisdom, and opaque to those who do not. This is the wisdom tradition’s version of the Johannine “whoever is of the truth hears my voice” (John 18:37).
10: qechu musari ve-'al-kasef — “take my instruction and not silver.” The comparison of wisdom to precious metals is a sustained motif (8:10, 19; 3:14-15; 16:16). Musar (H4148, discipline/instruction, often painful) is paired with da'at (knowledge, H1847) as the two forms of wisdom acquisition: the formative discipline of instruction and the cognitive grasp of knowledge. The claim that these exceed silver (keseph) and gold (charuts, refined/choice gold) is a value-claim about the hierarchy of human goods.
11: ki tovah chokmah mipeniniym — “for wisdom is better than jewels.” Peninim (H6443) are the most precious gems — red corals or pearls (also in 3:15 and 31:10, where the excellent wife is “more precious than peninim”). The linking of the wisdom comparison to the excellent wife comparison is not accidental: the man who has wisdom has the equivalent of the best possible wife. Kol-chafatzim lo' yishvu-vah — “and nothing desirable compares to her”: the comprehensive claim.
12: 'ani chokmah shakhanti 'ormah — “I, wisdom, have made prudence my dwelling.” The verb shakan (to dwell, to tabernacle — the root of mishkan, the tabernacle of Moses) applied to wisdom’s co-habitation with 'ormah (prudence) describes an intimate domestic relationship. Wisdom and prudence share the same house. Da'at mezimmot emtza' — “and I find knowledge and discretion.” Mezimmot (H4209, discretion, prudent planning) is the wisdom capacity to plan carefully.
13: yir'at YHWH seno'-ra' — “the fear of the LORD is hatred of evil.” The definition of yir'at YHWH (fear of the LORD, the most common wisdom formula) here is behavioral rather than affective: it consists in hating evil (ra', H7451). The three parallel objects of hatred — ge'ah (pride), ga'on (arrogance), derekh ra' (the evil way), and peh tahpukhot (the perverse mouth) — identify the enemies of wisdom. Hatred of pride is the disposition that characterizes those who truly fear YHWH.
14: li 'etzah ve-tushiyah — “with me are counsel and wisdom.” Tushiyah (H8454) is a term for effective wisdom, sound judgment, or perhaps the deeper “abiding reality” (BDB) of wisdom — the stable foundation that makes it competent. The accumulation of wisdom-terms in vv14-16 ('etzah, tushiyah, binah, gevurah) amounts to a comprehensive claim that all forms of effective human thinking and power belong to wisdom.
15: bi melakhim yimloku — “by me kings reign.” The political application of wisdom claims that legitimate rule is founded on wisdom rather than on force or heredity. Choqeqim (rulers who decree, H2710) and rozenim (princes, H7336) are the two categories of human authority; both exercise their authority properly only through wisdom. The grounding of political order in divine wisdom is the OT’s consistent teaching (cf. Rom 13:1’s governance language has this wisdom tradition as background).
16: bi sarim yasoru — “by me princes govern.” The parallel to v15 extends the claim from kings to the wider class of officials and nobles. Kol shoftei tzedeq — “all just judges” — identifies the criterion of legitimate judicial authority as tzedeq (justice/righteousness): the judiciary that aligns with wisdom’s moral order is the genuinely authoritative judiciary.
17: 'ani 'ohavai 'ehav — “I love those who love me.” The reciprocal love-language of wisdom is striking: 'ahav (H157, love) is used mutually. Wisdom does not merely reward or recognize her seekers — she loves them. The parallel meshachamai yimtza'uni — “those who seek me earnestly will find me” — uses shachar (to seek at dawn, to seek diligently, H7836), the same verb that the seductress in 7:15 used to describe her diligent search for the young man. Wisdom claims back the vocabulary of diligent seeking for herself.
18: 'osher ve-khavod 'itti — “wealth and honor are with me.” Wisdom’s material claims are not ethereal: she offers 'osher (wealth, H6239) and kavod (honor/glory, H3519) as real goods that her presence brings. Hon 'atiq ve-tzedaqah — “enduring riches and righteousness”: 'atiq (H6276, enduring, ancient) qualifies the wealth as lasting rather than transient. The combination of material wealth and righteousness in wisdom’s offer is the OT’s consistent portrait of the flourishing life.
19: tov peri 'mitzahav ufaz — “my fruit is better than gold, even fine gold.” Paz (H6337, fine gold, refined gold) is the highest quality of gold in the OT vocabulary. The metaphor of wisdom as a tree bearing fruit was established in 3:18 (“she is a tree of life to those who grasp her”); here the fruit claim is made comparatively: wisdom’s fruit exceeds the highest material wealth. The MKT’s translation “my fruit” captures the organic growth-metaphor of wisdom’s productive output.
20: be-'orach tzedaqah 'ahalekh — “in the path of righteousness I walk.” Wisdom personalizes the path-metaphor: she herself walks the righteous path, which is why following her constitutes walking it. Derakhei mishpat — “in the ways of justice” — the MKT renders mishpat (H4941, justice, just ordinance) as its legal-relational meaning: the structuring of reality according to right relationship. Wisdom’s path is not merely good advice but the alignment of one’s life with the moral order of creation.
21: le-hanchil 'ohavai yesh — “giving a real inheritance to those who love me.” Yesh (H3426) means ‘substance, that which is’ — the concrete reality of existence as opposed to illusion or vanity. Wisdom’s gift to those who love her is substantial existence: 'otzaroteihem 'amalle' — “and filling their storehouses.” The image of filled treasuries as wisdom’s result is the OT’s way of saying that wisdom produces a life of genuine abundance, not the false abundance promised by the adulteress.
23: me-'olam nissakhti me-rosh — “I was set up from of old, from the beginning.” Nassakh (H5258 in Niphal = to be poured, established, installed) may draw on the imagery of a king’s installation; the parallel me-rosh (from the beginning) and the preceding verse’s claim to pre-creational existence make this the formal claim to eternal appointment. Before the first act of creation, wisdom was installed in her office. The parallel with Ps 2:6 (“I have installed my king on Zion”) suggests a royal installation metaphor.
24: be-ein tehomot cholalti — “when there were no depths, I was brought forth.” Cholal (H2342 Polal = to be brought forth, to writhe in labor — a birthing verb) introduces the birth metaphor that dominates vv24-25: wisdom is not merely appointed (v23) but born. Tehomot (the deep waters, H8415 — the term of Gen 1:2’s tehom) are absent at wisdom’s birth; the primordial ocean has not yet been created when wisdom comes into being. The absence of springs (niv'ei maim) and the unformed mountains confirm this pre-creational setting.
25: lifnei gevaot chollalti — “before the hills, I was brought forth.” The birth-verb cholal repeats from v24, framing vv24-25 as a birth-diptych. Gevaot (hills, H1389) before they existed: the mountain landscape of the earth is post-wisdom in terms of temporal priority. The hills that are among the most ancient features of the physical world are themselves newer than wisdom’s coming-into-being.
26: 'ad lo' 'asah 'eretz ve-chutzot — “before he had made the earth and the fields.” The verse catalogs what had not yet been made at wisdom’s appearance: the earth ('eretz), the open fields (chutzot), and the beginning of the dust of the world (ro'sh 'afrot tevel). Tevel (H8398) is the inhabited world, the productive earth — its first dust is described as younger than wisdom. The comprehensiveness of the pre-creational claim reaches its fullest expression in this verse.
27: be-hakino shamayim sham 'ani — “when he established the heavens, I was there.” The eyewitness claim (“I was there”) sets wisdom as the observer-participant of creation. Chug (H2329, circle/vault — the circle drawn on the face of the deep) is the term for the vault of the sky; wisdom watches the creative act of inscribing the cosmic circle. The combination of “was there” and the active watching establishes wisdom as not merely pre-existent but present-at-creation.
28: be-'ammetz shechaqim mima'al — “when he made the skies firm above.” 'Ametz (H553, to make strong, confirm) applied to the clouds above establishes the structural stability of the atmosphere; 'ain 'ain tehom — “when the fountains of the deep were fixed.” The tehom of v24 reappears: the deep that did not exist when wisdom was born is now being organized and fixed. Wisdom witnesses the taming of the primordial waters.
29: be-sumu la-yam chuqo — “when he set a boundary for the sea.” Choq (H2706, the fixed boundary, the statute/decree) applied to the sea is the decree-metaphor for the order imposed on chaos: the waters must not transgress their boundary (pi lo' ya'avru — “that the waters should not transgress his command”). Be-chuqqo mosedei 'aretz — “when he marked out the foundations of the earth.” The same decree-term (choqo) governs both sea and earth-foundation: creation is ordered by statutory decree.
30: 'ehyeh 'etzlo 'amon — “I was beside him, the master craftsman.” 'Amon (H525) is the most debated term in Proverbs 8. The two primary options are: (a) 'amon as craftsman/artisan/skilled worker (cognate with Akkadian ummanu, ‘craftsman’; cf. Jer 52:15 use of the related 'aman); (b) 'amon as ward/nursling, the little child being reared (cf. Num 11:12; the MT pointing would require 'amun for this reading). The MKT adopts ‘master craftsman’ as fitting the active participatory role in creation. Va-'ehyeh sha'ashu'im yom yom — “I was his daily delight”: sha'ashu'im (H8191, delights/joys) is a plural of intensity; wisdom is what delights YHWH in the act of creation.
31: mesacheqet be-tevel 'artzo — “rejoicing in the world he had made.” Sachaq (to play, rejoice, dance — used of children at play and of David dancing before the ark in 2 Sam 6:21) describes wisdom’s disposition at creation as joyful delight in the work. Ve-sha'ashu'ai 'et bene 'adam — “my delight was in the children of humanity.” The final clause shifts wisdom’s delight from the creation (tevel) to the creature (bene 'adam): the world delights wisdom, but the human beings in it are the specific object of her delight. This is the wisdom tradition’s most direct statement of divine love for humanity as such.
32: ve-'ata banim shim'u-li — “and now, sons, listen to me.” The transition from the cosmic Wisdom-poem (vv22-31) back to direct address is abrupt and deliberate: the eternal and cosmic character of wisdom makes the father’s instruction more urgent, not less. The word 'ashrei (blessed, H835 — the same term that opens the Psalter in Ps 1:1) appears twice in vv32-34, framing the invitation to wisdom as access to the blessed life that the Psalter describes.
33: shim'u musar ve-chakemu — “hear instruction and be wise.” Musar (H4148, discipline, instruction through correction) is the term for the formative, often painful, process of wisdom acquisition. Ve-'al-tifrau' — “and do not reject it.” The negative command (para', H6544, to let loose, to neglect, to become unruly) frames wisdom-rejection as a loosening of the formation that wisdom provides — the wisdom tradition’s anthropology of instruction as constraint that liberates.
34: 'ashrei 'adam shome'a li — “blessed is the man who listens to me.” The second 'ashrei clause specifies the practice: daily attendance at wisdom’s doors (meshoqed 'al-daltotai yom yom, “watching daily at my doors”). Meshaqed (H8245, to watch, to keep vigil, to be sleepless) is the intensity-verb of diligent waiting; wisdom’s blessing is given to those who wait at her threshold every day. The image of wisdom’s house with gates and doorposts anticipates ch 9’s Wisdom-house with its seven pillars.
35: ki motz'i matza' chayyim — “for the one who finds me finds life.” Motze'i (to find, to encounter) and chayyim (life, H2416) form the central equation of Proverbs’ wisdom theology: wisdom = life. Va-yafoq ratzon mei-YHWH — “and obtains favor from the LORD”: ratzon (H7522, acceptance, favor, will) from YHWH is the theological goal toward which wisdom mediates. Finding wisdom is finding life; finding life is finding YHWH’s favor. The three terms (wisdom, life, divine favor) are presented as a single package.
36: ve-chot'i chomes nafsho — “but he who misses me harms himself.” Chata' (H2398) is typically ‘to sin’ but here means ‘to miss, to fail to hit the target’ — the concrete, pre-theological meaning of the sin vocabulary. The one who misses wisdom does violence (chamas, H2554) to his own life (nafsho). Kol-mesana'ai 'ahavu mavet — “all those who hate me love death.” The antithesis of the love-and-find-life equation (v35): hatred of wisdom is equivalent to love of death. The stark binary of wisdom/life versus folly/death is the pervasive structure of Proverbs’ moral universe.
